Because the big idea of what these APIs are going to do for us is that in theory instead of programming against the low level like C API, like these things, instead we could program against these technically database system agnostic APIs.
And then if we decided to change what database system we want to use, we would have to change our any other application code.
Of course that's not entirely true if you're writing raw SQL because as we said many times the SQL dialect could be different from one system to the next but we can ignore that.
So the history for this goes back into the late 80s early 90s.
Basically prior to something like JDBC it was just these C libraries that all the various database system vendors provided.
So things weren't portable. You would write again to a low level API talk to the database system that was very specific to the one database system you were using.

And so there was early people identify early on that would be nice that people writing a lot of applications would be nice to have a standard way to do database connectivity and to send queries to get back results.
So I think the first attempt was in the late 80s from side base they had something generically called DB library that was meant to be like an open source standard.
I don't know if I actually have the sort of it. It meant to be the standard everyone can implement.
But that didn't go anywhere. And then Microsoft teamed up with this other company called Stimma Technologies in the early 1990s and they put forth this thing called EDBC.
And so now pretty much every database system that you can think of today is going to have an EDBC implementation.
Even if it's actually not a relational database system and doesn't support SQL like MongoDB has an EDBC implementation.
Because again, it's something you have to put in the query command that you want to send over and EDBC doesn't know doesn't care whether it's SQL or not.
Just here's the thing I need to send to the server. But there's other APIs that are like iterative results sets, bind parameters to values and so forth or values to parameters and so forth.

So the high level looks like this. So the EDBC is based on the device driver model. It's sort of similar how like the hardware works in PCs where you know if you buy like a graphics card, the vendor that sell you to the graphic card, they're also going to provide you with a driver that you can install in your OS made about to communicate with the hardware.
So the same idea, the database system vendor is going to be responsible for providing you with a driver that you can use based on the ODBC spec, then then communicate with the database server.

So the application wants to run some queries on the database. They go through the ODBC driver and then the ODBC driver is responsible for sending the request over to the database server, getting back the result and then marshaling it back into the form that's required by the ODBC spec, then expose it to your application.

So this can mean things like if my client is expecting everything to be 32 bit integers, but the database server sends back 64 bit integers, then the drivers are starting for converting that and cleaning things up.
It also can do other things like there's certain features that are in the database server.
